        {
          "id": "intro-coding",
          "title": "What is Coding-Decoding?",
          "content": "Coding-Decoding is a reasoning skill where letters, numbers, or symbols are transformed using a hidden rule. Your task is to crack the logic. This is one of the most scoring topics in campus placement exams like TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Accenture.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Why Companies Ask",
              "content": "Coding-decoding tests your ability to find patterns quickly. In IT jobs, you constantly understand how data transforms from one form to another. This shows recruiters you can: find hidden rules, apply rules consistently, and work systematically under pressure."
            },
            {
              "title": "Basic Structure",
              "content": "You get a word and its code. Example: CAT is coded as FDW. Find the pattern, then either code another word or decode a given code. All questions follow: find the rule, apply the rule."
            },
            {
              "title": "Real-Life Example",
              "content": "Think of a secret language where every letter shifts by +3 (A->D, B->E). When friend sends FDW, you decode it as CAT. Companies test how fast you learn the secret language."
            },
            {
              "title": "Success Strategy",
              "content": "Step 1: Write A=1 to Z=26 on rough paper. Step 2: Compare each original letter with its code. Step 3: Note the difference per position. Step 4: Apply same pattern to new word. Step 5: Verify at least 2 letters before finalizing."
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "letter-coding-types",
          "title": "Letter Coding Types",
          "content": "Letter coding transforms letters into other letters. This is the most common category.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Forward Shift Coding",
              "content": "Each letter moves forward by fixed number. Example: CAT->FDW (+3 each). C+3=F, A+3=D, T+3=W. Common shifts: +1, +2, +3, +5. If wrapping: Z+1=A (circular alphabet)."
            },
            {
              "title": "Backward Shift Coding",
              "content": "Each letter moves backward. Example: DOG->ALD (-3). D-3=A, O-3=L, G-3=D. For wrapping: A-1=Z."
            },
            {
              "title": "Mirror/Reverse Coding",
              "content": "A<->Z, B<->Y, C<->X. Formula: new_pos = 27 - old_pos. A(1) becomes Z(26). This is like seeing the alphabet in a mirror."
            },
            {
              "title": "Alternating Pattern Coding",
              "content": "Different letters get different shifts. Example: LAP->OBS: L+3=O, A+1=B, P+3=S. Always check 2+ letter pairs to identify the alternating rule."
            },
            {
              "title": "Group Coding",
              "content": "Groups of letters coded together. Example: AB->CD, EF->GH. Or reverse pairs: AB->BA, CD->DC."
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "number-coding",
          "title": "Number and Mixed Coding",
          "content": "Numbers represent letters through position values. Letters and numbers can mix.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Direct Position Coding",
              "content": "Write the position number: A=1, B=2... CAT = 3-1-20 = 3120. Very common in TCS and Infosys."
            },
            {
              "title": "Sum/Product of Positions",
              "content": "Positions are added or multiplied. CAT = 3+1+20 = 24 (single number). Check if code length matches word (concatenated) or shorter (sum)."
            },
            {
              "title": "Digit Manipulation",
              "content": "Multiple operations on digits: sum (12->3), reverse (23->32), product (12->2)."
            },
            {
              "title": "Symbol-Based Coding",
              "content": "Symbols like star, circle, #, $ represent letters/numbers. Straightforward substitution."
            },
            {
              "title": "Mixed Letter-Number Coding",
              "content": "Code has both letters and numbers. Example: GO = G(7)+O(15) = 715. Just concatenate positions."
            }

        },
        {

              "title": "Vowel-Consonant Coding",

        {
          "id": "coding-tricks",
          "title": "Speed Shortcuts",
          "content": "Solve coding-decoding in 15-25 seconds.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "EJOTY Trick",
              "content": "E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25. Use nearest anchor. Need S? S is 1 before T(20)=19. Need G? G is 2 after E(5)=7."
            },
            {
              "title": "The 27-Minus Trick",
              "content": "For mirror coding: 27 - position. Memorize one formula. A(1) -> 27-1=26=Z. M(13) -> 27-13=14=N."
            },
            {
              "title": "Pattern Detection Speed",
              "content": "Check first AND last letter pairs first. If same shift, rule is uniform. If different, check for alternating."
            },
            {
              "title": "Two-Letter Verification",
              "content": "Never guess from one pair. Always check at least two letter pairs. If they match, you are confident."
            },
            {
              "title": "Reverse Check for Decoding",
              "content": "To decode, reverse the rule. If coding was +3, decoding is -3. Mirror (27-pos) is its own reverse!"
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "common-mistakes",
          "title": "Common Mistakes",
          "content": "Learn these to avoid losing easy marks.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Wrong Direction",
              "content": "If coding adds +3, decoding subtracts -3. Always check: original to code or code to original? Write arrows!"
            },
            {
              "title": "Forgetting Wrap Around",
              "content": "Z+1 = A (not Z). A-1 = Z (not A). Use circular alphabet: after Z comes A, before A is Z."
            },
            {
              "title": "Miscounting Positions",
              "content": "Use EJOTY anchors or write positions. Quick check: A=1, E=5, I=9, O=15, U=21."
            },
            {
              "title": "Assuming Uniform Pattern",
              "content": "Not all codes use same shift. Check 2-3 pairs. Look for vowel/consonant distinction or position-based rules."

              "title": "Skipping Verification",
              "content": "Take 5 seconds to verify one letter pair. If the code matches your rule, you are correct. One quick check saves 5 wrong answers."

          "explanation": "Foundation of all coding-decoding. Each letter has a fixed position. Memorize using EJOTY trick: E=5, J=10, O=15, T=20, Y=25.",
          "example": "CAT = C(3)A(1)T(20) = 3120"
        },

          "explanation": "Each letter shifts forward by a fixed number. Same shift for all or alternating.",
          "example": "CAT -> FDW (+3 each): C+3=F, A+3=D, T+3=W"
        },

          "id": "hcf-lcm",
          "title": "HCF & LCM Made Easy",
          "content": "HCF finds the biggest common divisor. LCM finds the smallest common multiple. They are two sides of the same coin.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Finding HCF (GCD)",
              "content": "Step 1: Prime factorize each number. Step 2: Pick common primes with LOWEST powers. Step 3: Multiply them. Example: HCF of 24 ($2^3 \\times 3$) and 36 ($2^2 \\times 3^2$). Common primes: 2 and 3. Lowest powers: $2^2$ and $3^1$. HCF $= 4 \\times 3 = 12$."
            },
            {
              "title": "Finding LCM",
              "content": "Step 1: Prime factorize each number. Step 2: Pick ALL primes with HIGHEST powers. Step 3: Multiply them. Example: LCM of 24 ($2^3 \\times 3$) and 36 ($2^2 \\times 3^2$). Pick $2^3$ and $3^2$. LCM $= 8 \\times 9 = 72$."
            },
            {
              "title": "The Golden Formula",
              "content": "For any two numbers $a$ and $b$: $a \\times b = \\text{HCF}(a,b) \\times \\text{LCM}(a,b)$. If you know any three values, find the fourth. Example: If product is 288 and HCF is 12, then LCM $= 288 \\div 12 = 24$."
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "cyclicity",
          "title": "The Last Digit Trick (Cyclicity)",
          "content": "The unit digit of powers follows a repeating pattern called cyclicity. This is one of the most tested concepts in placement exams.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Always Same (Cyclicity 1)",
              "content": "Digits 0, 1, 5, 6: These never change. $5^{100}$ ends in 5. $6^{50}$ ends in 6. $1^{1000}$ ends in 1."
            },
            {
              "title": "Alternating (Cyclicity 2)",
              "content": "Digits 4 and 9 switch between two values. For 4: $4^{\\text{odd}} = 4$, $4^{\\text{even}} = 6$. For 9: $9^{\\text{odd}} = 9$, $9^{\\text{even}} = 1$."
            },
            {
              "title": "Four-Step Cycle (Cyclicity 4)",
              "content": "Digits 2, 3, 7, 8 repeat every 4 powers. Cycles: 2: $[2,4,8,6]$, 3: $[3,9,7,1]$, 7: $[7,9,3,1]$, 8: $[8,4,2,6]$."
            },
            {
              "title": "How to Solve Any Cyclicity Problem",
              "content": "Step 1: Take the unit digit of the base number. Step 2: Find its cycle length (1, 2, or 4). Step 3: Divide the exponent by the cycle length. Step 4: Use the remainder to pick from the cycle. Remainder 0 means the last element. Example: Unit digit of $7^{345}$. Cycle of 7: $[7,9,3,1]$, length 4. $345 \\div 4 = 86$ remainder 1. 1st element is 7. Answer: 7."
            }
          ]
        },
        {
          "id": "remainders",
          "title": "Remainder Theorems (Level-Up Shortcuts)",
          "content": "Master these theorems to solve remainder problems in seconds. Each theorem has a specific trigger that tells you when to use it.",
          "subsections": [
            {
              "title": "Basic Algebraic Remainder",
              "content": "Trigger: Expression like $(ax + b)^n$ divided by $a$. Shortcut: Ignore the $ax$ term, just compute $b^n \\div a$. Example: Remainder of $10^{20} \\div 9$. Write $10 = 9+1$. Then $(9+1)^{20}$: every term except $1^{20}$ has factor 9. Remainder $= 1^{20} = 1$."
            },
            {
              "title": "Fermat's Little Theorem",
              "content": "Trigger: Large exponent with a PRIME modulus. Theorem: If $p$ is prime and $a$ not divisible by $p$, then $a^{p-1} \\equiv 1 \\pmod{p}$. Example: $3^{100} \\bmod 7$. Since 7 is prime, $3^6 \\equiv 1 \\pmod{7}$. $100 = 6 \\times 16 + 4$, so $3^{100} \\equiv 3^4 = 81 \\equiv 4 \\pmod{7}$."
            },
            {
              "title": "Euler's Theorem",
              "content": "Trigger: Large exponent with a COMPOSITE modulus (and base and modulus are co-prime). Theorem: If $\\gcd(a,n) = 1$, then $a^{\\phi(n)} \\equiv 1 \\pmod{n}$. Example: $2^{100} \\bmod 15$. $\\gcd(2,15)=1$. $\\phi(15) = 15(1-1/3)(1-1/5) = 8$. $2^8 \\equiv 1 \\pmod{15}$. $100 = 8\\times12+4$, so $2^{100} \\equiv 2^4 = 16 \\equiv 1 \\pmod{15}$."
            },
            {
              "title": "Chinese Remainder Theorem",
              "content": "Trigger: A number has different remainders with different divisors. Problem: Find $x$ such that $x \\equiv 1 \\pmod{3}$ and $x \\equiv 2 \\pmod{5}$. Step 1: For small numbers, just try values. Numbers $\\equiv 1$ mod 3: 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, 16... Check mod 5: 1$\\to$1 (no), 4$\\to$4 (no), 7$\\to$2 (yes!). Answer: $x = 7$. The general solution is $x = 7 + 15k$ (since $3 \\times 5 = 15$)."
            }
          ]

          "formula": "x \\equiv \\sum_{i=1}^k r_i M_i y_i \\pmod{M}",
          "explanation": "Given $x \\equiv r_i \\pmod{m_i}$ with $\\gcd(m_i,m_j)=1$, let $M = \\prod m_i$, $M_i = M/m_i$, and $y_i$ be the modular inverse of $M_i$ modulo $m_i$ (i.e., $M_i y_i \\equiv 1 \\pmod{m_i}$). Then the unique solution modulo $M$ is $x \\equiv \\sum r_i M_i y_i \\pmod{M}$.",
          "example": "Solve $x \\equiv 1 \\pmod{3}$, $x \\equiv 2 \\pmod{5}$. $M=15$, $M_1=5$, $M_2=3$. $5y_1 \\equiv 1 \\pmod{3} \\to y_1=2$. $3y_2 \\equiv 1 \\pmod{5} \\to y_2=2$. $x = 1\\times5\\times2 + 2\\times3\\times2 = 10+12 = 22 \\equiv 7 \\pmod{15}$. Smallest $x=7$."

          "title": "Least Number to Multiply or Divide for Perfect Square",
          "formula": "\\text{Multiply/Divide so each prime exponent becomes even}",
          "explanation": "To make a number a perfect square: prime factorize, then for each prime with an odd exponent, multiply by that prime (to make exponent even) or divide by that prime (to remove odd power). The least number to multiply is the product of all primes with odd exponents. The least number to divide is the product of all primes with odd exponents (removing one power of each).",
          "example": "$72 = 2^3 \\times 3^2$. Exponents: $2^3$ has odd exponent 3, $3^2$ has even exponent 2. To make perfect square: multiply by $2$ to get $2^4 \\times 3^2 = 144 = 12^2$. Or divide by $2$ to get $2^2 \\times 3^2 = 36 = 6^2$. Least multiplier $= 2$, least divisor $= 2$."

(4) For $i = 5$ to $\\sqrt{n}$, check if $n$ is divisible by $i$ or $i+2$. If none divide, $n$ is prime.     {
      "id": "bodmas",
      "title": "The BODMAS / VBODMAS Rule",
      "content": "When you see a long expression with mixed operators (+, -, *, /) and brackets ( ), { }, [ ], and the word 'of', use BODMAS. This is the #1 most tested rule in TCS, Infosys, Wipro, and Cognizant placement papers. Companies love to place 'of' right before multiplication to trap students. Remember: 'Of' comes BEFORE Division and Multiplication.",
      "subsections": [
        {
          "title": "The Order (VBODMAS)",
          "content": "Step 1: V = Vinculum (bar bracket $\\overline{a-b}$). Solve this FIRST. Step 2: B = Brackets ( ), { }, [ ] from inner to outer. Step 3: O = Of (fractions/percentages or literal 'of'). Step 4: D = Division. Step 5: M = Multiplication. Step 6: A = Addition. Step 7: S = Subtraction. Note: Division/Multiplication have same priority, solve left to right. Same for Addition/Subtraction."
        },
        {
          "title": "Pattern Recognition - How to Spot BODMAS Questions",
          "content": "Pattern: A long string of numbers with multiple operators like $12 + 8 \\div 4 \\times 3 - 5$. Trap Pattern: 'Of' next to division, like $24 \\div 6 \\text{ of } 2 + 5$. If you do division first, you get the wrong answer! Always do 'Of' first."
        },
        {
          "title": "Common Mistakes in Placement Exams",
          "content": "Mistake 1: Doing division before 'of'. Rule: 'Of' is like a hidden multiplication that must be solved first. Mistake 2: Solving addition before subtraction when subtraction comes first (left to right rule). Mistake 3: Forgetting vinculum/bar bracket which has highest priority. Example: $\\overline{9-3}$ must be solved before anything else."
        },
        {
          "title": "Step-by-Step BODMAS Problem Solving",
          "content": "Problem: $12 + 8 \\div 4 \\times 3 - 5$. Step 1 (Division): $8 \\div 4 = 2$, expression: $12 + 2 \\times 3 - 5$. Step 2 (Multiplication): $2 \\times 3 = 6$, expression: $12 + 6 - 5$. Step 3 (Add/Sub left to right): $12 + 6 = 18$, $18 - 5 = 13$. Answer: 13."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "surds-indices",
      "title": "Surds & Indices (Laws of Exponents)",
      "content": "Indices questions appear in almost every placement paper. The key pattern: variables in the exponent position like $2^x = 32$, or fractional powers like $27^{2/3}$. The golden rule: Convert everything to the SAME BASE. If you cannot, use the laws of exponents.",
      "subsections": [
        {
          "title": "The 6 Core Laws of Indices",
          "content": "Law 1: $a^m \\times a^n = a^{m+n}$ (Product: add powers). Law 2: $a^m \\div a^n = a^{m-n}$ (Quotient: subtract powers). Law 3: $(a^m)^n = a^{mn}$ (Power of power: multiply). Law 4: $a^0 = 1$ (any non-zero base). Law 5: $a^{-m} = 1/a^m$ (negative power = reciprocal). Law 6: $a^{m/n} = (\\sqrt[n]{a})^m$ (fractional power = root then power)."
        },
        {
          "title": "Pattern Recognition for Indices",
          "content": "Pattern 1: Same base different exponents $\\rightarrow$ use Law 1 or 2. Pattern 2: Different base same exponent $\\rightarrow$ try to convert to same base. Pattern 3: Fractional exponents $\\rightarrow$ use Law 6 (root-power). Pattern 4: Negative exponents $\\rightarrow$ use Law 5 (reciprocal). Pattern 5: Complex expression like $(x^a/x^b)^{a^2+ab+b^2} \\times ... $ $\\rightarrow$ use Law 3 and algebraic identities."
        },
        {
          "title": "Common Indices Tricks",
          "content": "Trick 1: $256^{0.16} \\times 256^{0.09}$. Add powers: $0.16+0.09=0.25=1/4$. So $256^{1/4} = 4$. Trick 2: For $(a/b)^{x-1} = (b/a)^{x-3}$, flip the fraction: $(a/b)^{x-1} = (a/b)^{-(x-3)} = (a/b)^{3-x}$. So $x-1 = 3-x$, giving $x=2$."
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "roots",
      "title": "Square Roots & Cube Roots (Speed Shortcuts)",
      "content": "When asked to find $\\sqrt{X}$ or $\\sqrt[3]{X}$ where X is a 4 to 6 digit perfect square/cube, NEVER use long division. Use the unit-digit shortcut method. This saves 30+ seconds per question.",
      "subsections": [
        {
          "title": "Square Root Unit Digit Shortcut",
          "content": "Memorize: Ends in 1 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 1 or 9. Ends in 4 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 2 or 8. Ends in 5 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 5. Ends in 6 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 4 or 6. Ends in 9 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 3 or 7. Ends in 0 $\\rightarrow$ root ends in 0. Ends in 2,3,7,8 $\\rightarrow$ NOT a perfect square! To find tens digit: ignore last 2 digits, find which perfect squares the remaining part falls between, pick the smaller number."
        },
        {
          "title": "Cube Root Unit Digit Shortcut",
          "content": "Memorize: 1$\\to$1, 2$\\to$8, 3$\\to$7, 4$\\to$4, 5$\\to$5, 6$\\to$6, 7$\\to$3, 8$\\to$2, 9$\\to$9, 0$\\to$0. To find tens digit: group digits in threes from right, ignore last group, find cube of remaining part."
        },
        {
          "title": "The Check Trick for Square Roots",
          "content": "After finding tens digit (say T), compute $T \\times (T+1)$. If remaining part (ignored last 2 digits) is greater than this product, pick the larger unit digit. If smaller, pick the smaller unit digit. Example: $\\sqrt{5776}$: tens digit=7, $7\\times8=56$. Remaining=57. 57>56, so pick larger unit digit 6. Answer: 76."
        }
      ]
    },

    {
      "id": "bodmas-basic",
      "title": "BODMAS Order of Operations",
      "formula": "\\text{V} \\to \\text{B} \\to \\text{O} \\to \\text{D/M} \\to \\text{A/S} \\quad (\\text{left to right within D/M and A/S})",
      "explanation": "The VBODMAS rule defines the priority order: Vinculum (bar bracket) first, then Brackets from inner to outer, then Of, then Division and Multiplication (equal priority, left to right), then Addition and Subtraction (equal priority, left to right). This is the foundation of all simplification.",
      "example": "$12+8\\div4\\times3-5$: Step 1: $8\\div4=2$ (D). Step 2: $2\\times3=6$ (M). Step 3: $12+6=18$ (A). Step 4: $18-5=13$ (S). Answer: 13."
    },
    {
      "id": "bodmas-of",
      "title": "The 'Of' Rule in BODMAS",
      "formula": "a \\text{ of } b = a \\times b \\quad \\text{(solved before other D/M)}",
      "explanation": "'Of' acts like multiplication but has HIGHER priority than regular division and multiplication. This is the most common trap in placement exams. Whenever you see 'of', solve that multiplication FIRST before any other division or multiplication in the expression.",
      "example": "$24\\div6\\text{ of }2+5$: Step 1: $6\\text{ of }2=12$ (O). Step 2: $24\\div12=2$ (D). Step 3: $2+5=7$ (A). Answer: 7. (Wrong method: $24\\div6=4$, $4\\times2=8$, $8+5=13$.)"
    },
    {
      "id": "bodmas-vinculum",
      "title": "Vinculum / Bar Bracket Rule",
      "formula": "\\overline{a-b} = a-b \\quad \\text{(solved before all other brackets)}",
      "explanation": "Vinculum (the bar over an expression) has the HIGHEST priority in VBODMAS. Solve everything under the bar before solving any other brackets. This is often tested in TCS and Infosys exams with nested brackets.",
      "example": "$25-[16-\\{14-(12-\\overline{9-3})\\}]$: Step 1: $\\overline{9-3}=6$. Step 2: $(12-6)=6$. Step 3: $\\{14-6\\}=8$. Step 4: $[16-8]=8$. Step 5: $25-8=17$. Answer: 17."

      "id": "indices-multiply",
      "title": "Law of Indices: Multiplication",
      "formula": "a^m \\times a^n = a^{m+n}",
      "explanation": "When multiplying terms with the SAME base, ADD the exponents. This is the most frequently used law. Applied when you see expressions like $5^x \\times 5^y$ or $256^{0.16} \\times 256^{0.09}$.",
      "example": "$256^{0.16} \\times 256^{0.09} = 256^{0.25} = 256^{1/4} = 4$. Step 1: Add powers $0.16+0.09=0.25=1/4$. Step 2: $256^{1/4}$ means 4th root of $256 = 4$."
    },
    {

      "title": "Law of Indices: Division",
      "formula": "a^m \\div a^n = a^{m-n}",
      "explanation": "When dividing terms with the SAME base, SUBTRACT the exponents (numerator exponent minus denominator exponent). Used for expressions like $5^{x+3}/5^{x-1}$ or $(x^a)/(x^b)$.",
      "example": "$5^{x+3} \\div 5^{x-1} = 5^{(x+3)-(x-1)} = 5^{4} = 625$. Step 1: Subtract powers: $(x+3)-(x-1)=x+3-x+1=4$. Step 2: $5^4=625$."

      "title": "Synonym Relationship Rule",
      "formula": "A ~ B => C ~ ?",
      "explanation": "When A and B have similar meanings, find a word with similar meaning to C.",
      "example": "Happy : Joyful :: Sad : Unhappy"

  "categoryTitle": "Verbal & English",
  "categoryIcon": "A",
  "categoryColor": "#059669",
  "categorySubtitle": "Verbal Reasoning, Analogies & Coding-Decoding",
  "learningPath": [
    "intro-analogies",
    "word-analogies",
    "letter-number-analogies",
    "pattern-recognition",
    "company-patterns",
    "shortcuts-speed"
  ],
  "modules": [
    {
      "id": "intro-analogies",
      "title": "What is an Analogy?",
      "type": "concept",
      "order": 1,
      "content": {
        "introduction": "An analogy is a comparison between two things that shows how they are similar. Companies use analogy questions to test your reasoning, vocabulary, and pattern recognition skills. These appear in TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Accenture and almost all campus placement exams.",
        "subsections": [
          {
            "title": "Why Companies Ask Analogy Questions",
            "content": "Companies want people who think logically and spot patterns quickly. Solving analogies proves you can: (1) Understand relationships between concepts, (2) Think abstractly, (3) Make quick decisions under pressure. These are essential IT job skills."
          },
          {
            "title": "Basic Structure",
            "content": "An analogy looks like: A : B :: C : ? (read as A is to B as C is to what). Your job is to find the relationship between A and B, then apply it to C. Example: Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ? Answer: School (a doctor works in a hospital, a teacher works in a school)."
          },
          {
            "title": "Real-Life Example",
            "content": "If your friend says My phone is to me what wings are to a bird, you understand the phone is essential for communication just like wings are essential for flight. You are comparing two relationships that work the same way."
          },
          {
            "title": "How to Approach",
            "content": "Step 1: Read the first pair carefully. Step 2: Identify the exact relationship. Step 3: Apply the same relationship to C. Step 4: Find the matching answer. Step 5: Double-check by reversing the logic."
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "word-analogies",
      "title": "Word Analogies (Most Common)",
      "type": "concept",
      "order": 2,
      "content": {
        "introduction": "Word analogies use regular English words. The relationship can be based on meaning, function, category, or association.",
        "subsections": [
          {
            "title": "Synonym Analogies (Same Meaning)",
            "content": "The relationship is same meaning. Example: Happy : Joyful :: Sad : ? Answer: Unhappy. Common pairs: Big-Large, Small-Tiny, Fast-Quick, Brave-Courageous, Afraid-Scared."
          },
          {
            "title": "Antonym Analogies (Opposite Meaning)",
            "content": "The relationship is opposite meaning. Example: Hot : Cold :: Light : ? Answer: Dark. Common pairs: Up-Down, Good-Bad, Happy-Sad, Rich-Poor, Strong-Weak."
          },
          {
            "title": "Cause and Effect Analogies",
            "content": "One word causes the other. Example: Rain : Flood :: Virus : ? Answer: Disease. Ask yourself: Does A cause B?"
          },
          {
            "title": "Function/Use Analogies",
            "content": "Shows what something is used for. Example: Pen : Write :: Knife : ? Answer: Cut. Pattern: A is used to B."
          },
          {
            "title": "Part to Whole Analogies",
            "content": "First word is part of the second. Example: Finger : Hand :: Toe : ? Answer: Foot. More: Page : Book, Wheel : Car, Leaf : Tree."
          },
          {
            "title": "Profession and Tool",
            "content": "Shows what tool a professional uses. Example: Doctor : Stethoscope :: Carpenter : ? Answer: Hammer. Common: Teacher-Chalk, Chef-Knife, Painter-Brush."
          },
          {
            "title": "Animal and Young Ones",
            "content": "Shows parent and baby relationship. Example: Cat : Kitten :: Dog : ? Answer: Puppy. Memorize: Cow-Calf, Lion-Cub, Sheep-Lamb, Horse-Foal."
          },
          {
            "title": "Animal and Home/Dwelling",
            "content": "Shows where an animal lives. Example: Dog : Kennel :: Bird : ? Answer: Nest. Learn: Lion-Den, Rabbit-Burrow, Bee-Hive, Horse-Stable."
          },
          {
            "title": "Country and Capital",
            "content": "Shows capital cities. Example: India : Delhi :: USA : ? Answer: Washington DC. Must-know: India-Delhi, UK-London, France-Paris, Japan-Tokyo."
          },
          {
            "title": "Person and Achievement",
            "content": "Shows who created something. Example: Shakespeare : Hamlet :: Einstein : ? Answer: Theory of Relativity. Common: Newton-Gravity, Edison-Light Bulb."
          }
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "letter-number-analogies",
      "title": "Letter and Number Analogies",
      "type": "skill",
      "order": 3,
      "content": {
        "introduction": "Letter analogies use alphabet patterns and number analogies use mathematical relationships.",
        "subsections": [
          {
            "title": "Position-Based Letter Analogies",
